items.question_text: A facility has a written agreement with the local flight inspection unit that all traffic and intention exchanges will be handled through the operations supervisor rather than by direct controller-pilot contact. A flight inspection aircraft enters the sector. Must the controller maintain direct contact with the pilot?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, maintain direct contact and exchange traffic and intention information 'unless otherwise agreed to.' The written agreement creates an exception — the controller follows the agreed-upon alternative coordination method rather than direct pilot contact.

choices.text: No. The 'unless otherwise agreed to' qualifier permits an alternative coordination method when a written agreement exists.
Yes. Direct pilot contact is mandatory for all flight inspection operations regardless of agreements.
No, but only because flight inspection aircraft operating under an agreement are treated as routine IFR traffic.
Yes, unless the pilot specifically requests relay-only communication through the supervisor.

items.question_text: A flight inspection aircraft requests a clearance that conflicts with three departure releases scheduled in the next 4 minutes. The controller asks the pilot to accept a 10-minute delay so the departures can be released first. No emergency exists. Is the delay request permissible?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-2a, the controller must clear the aircraft 'according to pilot request as soon as practicable' and must not ask the pilot to deviate from planned action 'except to preclude an emergency situation.' A routine sequencing delay is not an emergency — the controller must clear the aircraft as requested rather than impose a delay for departure releases.

choices.text: Yes. Routine departure sequencing takes priority over flight inspection clearance requests.
No. A routine sequencing delay is not an emergency situation, so the controller must not ask the pilot to deviate from the planned action.
Yes, but only if the delay is 5 minutes or less and the pilot does not object.
No, but only because flight inspection aircraft have unconditional priority over all other traffic.

items.question_text: A military aircraft's flight plan does not contain 'dangerous cargo' or 'inert devices' in the remarks, but the pilot states 'inert devices' during initial radio contact. Does special handling for dangerous materials apply?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-1a, special handling applies when the words appear in the flight plan remarks OR when the pilot uses the words in radio communication. Either trigger alone activates the special handling requirement — both are not required.

choices.text: No. Both the flight plan remarks AND radio communication must contain the trigger words for special handling to apply.
No. Radio communication alone is never sufficient; the flight plan remarks must always contain the trigger words.
Yes. The pilot's use of the words in radio communication is sufficient to trigger special handling even without flight plan notation.
Yes, but only if the aircraft is also carrying materials classified as dangerous under military regulations.

items.question_text: A law enforcement aircraft conducting surveillance requests communication on a separate frequency. The sector has only one available frequency and is moderately busy. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a1, the controller must communicate with law enforcement aircraft on a separate and unique communications frequency 'whenever possible.' The qualifier 'whenever possible' means the controller is not required to create a separate frequency when resources do not permit it, but should do so when feasible.

choices.text: Refuse the request because a separate frequency is only available when the sector has no other traffic.
Immediately create a separate frequency by splitting the sector, regardless of staffing or equipment constraints.
Grant the request but instruct the law enforcement aircraft to monitor both frequencies simultaneously.
Provide a separate frequency whenever possible — if resources do not permit it, the obligation does not apply.

items.question_text: A supersonic flight plan above FL 600 is filed 10 hours before the proposed departure time. The originating ARTCC determines the route is straightforward and no special coordination is needed. Can the originating ARTCC waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14a, flight plans involving supersonic flight are required 16 hours in advance, but 'the originating ARTCC, where the flight plan is first filed, may waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement.' The waiver authority rests solely with the originating ARTCC.

choices.text: Yes. The originating ARTCC may waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement.
No. The 16-hour requirement is absolute and cannot be waived by any facility.
Yes, but only the Command Center can approve the waiver, not the originating ARTCC.
No. Waiver requires coordination with and approval from all ARTCCs along the route of flight.

items.question_text: A pilot's intended landing airfield is within a forecast hazardous radiation area. The controller informs the pilot of this. What must the controller then request from the pilot?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-16b, when an airfield of intended landing lies within a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area, the controller must inform the pilot AND 'request the pilot to advise his/her intentions.' The pilot retains decision authority on whether to land there.

choices.text: Request the pilot to divert to the nearest unaffected airfield immediately.
Request the pilot to advise his or her intentions regarding the landing.
Request the pilot to hold outside the area until the radiation hazard dissipates.
Request the pilot to declare an emergency before proceeding to the affected airfield.

items.question_text: A SAMP aircraft requests a climb from FL 280 to FL 340 for a sampling mission. The sector has moderate traffic at adjacent altitudes, and approving the request would require reclearing two other IFR aircraft. What authority does the controller have to accommodate the SAMP request?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, the controller must 'approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible' and 'other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that requests by SAMP aircraft are approved.' The controller has explicit authority to reclear other IFR traffic to prioritize SAMP mission requests.

choices.text: Deny the request because other IFR aircraft already occupy the adjacent altitudes and cannot be moved.
Approve the request only if the two other aircraft volunteer to change altitude without a controller-initiated reclearance.
Reclear the other IFR aircraft to accommodate the SAMP aircraft's request to the maximum extent possible.
Place the SAMP aircraft in a holding pattern until the other aircraft have cleared the requested altitudes on their own.

items.question_text: A stationary ALTRV has been issued for a space launch, creating an Aircraft Hazard Area (AHA). A small airport that normally receives IFR approach services lies within the AHA boundary. No real-time start/end notifications are being provided to the affected facility. What is the correct action?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-2d2, the controller must not provide ATC services to aircraft at airports that lie within an AHA unless real-time notifications of the actual start and end of activity are provided via ATO Space Operations coordination. Without those notifications, ATC services to that airport are suspended for the duration of the ALTRV.

choices.text: Continue providing normal IFR approach services to the airport since the ALTRV is for space operations, not aviation.
Provide ATC services only to IFR aircraft; VFR aircraft may operate without restriction.
Provide services only during the launch window itself; the airport may operate normally at all other times.
Do not provide ATC services to aircraft at airports within the AHA without real-time start/end notifications.

items.question_text: An LOA permits transit of a Restricted Area with using agency coordination. The using agency instructs you to clear the aircraft at 5,000 feet through the area, but you have nonparticipating IFR traffic at 5,000 feet that cannot be moved in time. What must you do?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-4b, if unable to comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances, clear the aircraft in accordance with 9-3-2, Separation Minima. This means treating the aircraft as a nonparticipant and applying the standard separation buffer from the SUA boundary rather than attempting a transit you cannot safely execute.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft in accordance with the nonparticipant separation minima in paragraph 9-3-2.
Clear the aircraft through the area at 5,000 feet anyway since the using agency has authorized the transit.
Hold the aircraft outside the area until the nonparticipating traffic has passed, then proceed with the using agency's instructions.
Request the using agency to temporarily deactivate the Restricted Area for the transit.

items.question_text: A civil-operated Air Force type aircraft requests radar vectoring to a jettison drop area. The controller checks facility records and finds no written agreement with this operator. Can vectoring service be provided?
items.explanation: Per 9-5-1, civil operators and civil aircraft may receive vectoring service for jettisoning external stores only when a written agreement is in effect. Unlike USAF/ANG/Air Force Reserve aircraft (which may receive service at any time), civil operators require the written agreement that relieves the FAA of liability.

choices.text: Yes. All aircraft carrying external stores may use the drop area regardless of military or civil status.
No. Civil operators require a written agreement to be in effect before vectoring service can be provided.
Yes, but only if the civil aircraft declares an emergency before jettisoning.
No. Jettison drop areas are only available to U.S. military aircraft in all circumstances.

items.question_text: A parachute operation is authorized in Class B airspace within the designated jump area. The controller observes that a developing weather cell will move through the jump area in approximately 15 minutes. What authority does the controller have?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2c, the controller may 'impose, as necessary, any conditions and restrictions which in your judgment would promote the safety of the operation.' This provides broad discretionary authority — the controller can restrict or condition the jump operation based on safety concerns such as approaching weather.

choices.text: No special authority beyond what the pilot requests — the controller must allow the jump to proceed as planned.
The controller may only impose restrictions when the jump aircraft explicitly asks for guidance on safety conditions.
Impose conditions and restrictions as necessary, including suspending the operation, based on the controller's safety judgment.
The controller must defer to the jump aircraft commander on all safety decisions and cannot independently restrict operations.

items.situation: A flight inspection aircraft on a recorded run is on a 10-mile final for a runway inspection. Another aircraft at a nearby airport declares an emergency — engine failure on takeoff — and needs to return to the runway immediately. The emergency aircraft will conflict with the flight inspection aircraft's final approach path.
items.question_text: What is the correct controller action regarding the flight inspection aircraft?

choices.text: Continue the recorded run and vector the emergency aircraft away from the runway.
Terminate the recorded run without coordinating with the flight inspection pilot.
Give the recorded run absolute priority and delay handling of the emergency aircraft.
Ask the flight inspection aircraft to deviate because the conflict must be resolved to preclude the emergency.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-2a, the controller must not ask the flight inspection pilot to deviate from planned action 'except to preclude an emergency situation.' The engine-failure emergency triggers this exception — the controller may ask the flight inspection aircraft to deviate from its planned approach path to avoid the emergency. The emergency exception is the sole basis for requesting a deviation.

items.situation: A supersonic aircraft above FL 600 is making a programmed 90-degree turn. Your trainee vectors the aircraft 10 degrees left of the planned turn path to avoid a conflict. The pilot reports the vector will cause the aircraft to miss its next fix and impact mission objectives.
items.question_text: Was the trainee's vectoring action appropriate?

choices.text: No. Supersonic aircraft turns are pre-designated and vectoring seriously affects range and mission objectives.
Yes. Vectoring supersonic aircraft is the standard separation method at all altitudes.
Yes. A 10-degree deviation is within the acceptable margin for supersonic turns above FL 600.
No, but only because the turn was less than 90 degrees — vectoring is permitted for turns of 90 degrees or more.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-14 and its note, supersonic aircraft turns of 90 degrees or more are pre-designated with entry and exit points in the flight plan, and 'vectoring supersonic aircraft seriously affects the range and mission objectives.' The preferred method is to descend the lower aircraft and use vertical separation, not vector the supersonic aircraft. Additionally, 9-2-14b requires that entry and exit points of turns of 90 degrees or more be designated in the route of flight.

items.situation: A USAF single-pilot jet aircraft formation operating at night requests the special use frequency. The formation will operate within airspace that has been assigned for a special military operation. A trainee assigns the special use frequency.
items.question_text: Is the trainee's frequency assignment correct?

choices.text: Yes. Single-pilot jet formations at night are always eligible for the special use frequency.
No. The special use frequency must not be assigned when the aircraft will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations.
Yes, but only if the formation leader specifically requests it and the frequency is not already in use.
No. Single-pilot formations are only eligible for the special use frequency during instrument weather conditions, not at night.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-15c, the special use frequency must not be assigned to the aircraft listed in subparagraph a1 when they will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations. The aircraft type and conditions (night single-pilot formation) normally qualify under a1, but the special-military-operations airspace prohibition overrides eligibility.

items.situation: A nonstandard cell formation of six military aircraft is approaching the breakup fix in your en route sector. After the planned breakup, three aircraft will continue eastbound at FL 280 and three will turn southbound descending to FL 240. The formation leader calls and reports they are ready for breakup.
items.question_text: What action must you take at the breakup fix?

choices.text: Instruct the formation to continue beyond the breakup fix as a single cell until they reach their destinations.
Transfer control to the next sector immediately without issuing breakup clearances.
Issue clearances authorizing transition from formation to the individual routes and altitudes for each group.
Tell the formation leader to coordinate the breakup directly with the next sector on the new frequency.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-21f, if the breakup occurs in your area, issue appropriate clearances to authorize transition from formation to individual routes or altitudes. The controller must actively issue the breakup clearances — the formation does not simply separate on its own. If a breakup cannot be approved, issue a clearance for the flight to continue as a formation.

items.situation: An aircraft is on an approved ALTRV that transits through a Warning Area. The ALTRV altitude is FL 250. The Warning Area is also designated as an ATCAA. A trainee tells you the aircraft should be treated as a nonparticipant because ATCAA airspace requires application of standard SUA separation procedures.

choices.text: Yes. All aircraft near an ATCAA are always treated as nonparticipants regardless of approved ALTRV status.
No. An approved ALTRV is an exception, but only when the airspace is a Warning Area — not when it is an ATCAA.
No. An approved ALTRV always creates an exception regardless of the type of airspace involved.
Yes. The approved ALTRV exception does not apply when the airspace in question is an ATCAA.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-1d, the aircraft-on-approved-ALTRV exception applies 'unless the airspace area in question is an ATCAA.' When the airspace is designated as an ATCAA, the approved ALTRV does not create an exception — the standard SUA separation procedures still apply. The trainee correctly identified that the ATCAA designation negates the ALTRV exception.

items.situation: An unmanned free balloon is drifting through your sector at an estimated altitude reported by the operator. A pilot requests separation from the balloon. The last confirmed altitude report from the operator is 30 minutes old. The balloon's transponder is operating intermittently. You have primary radar returns showing a target in approximately the expected position.
items.question_text: Can you provide vertical separation between the aircraft and the balloon?

choices.text: No. Do not attempt vertical separation without accurate balloon altitude information.
Yes. Use the operator's last reported altitude since it is the most recent data available.
Yes. Primary radar returns confirm the balloon's position, so vertical separation can be applied.
Yes. The pilot's concurrence with any separation instruction makes altitude accuracy irrelevant.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-1c, do not attempt to separate aircraft from the balloon by using vertical separation unless you have accurate balloon altitude information. A 30-minute-old report with intermittent transponder returns does not constitute accurate altitude information. The controller should use lateral or radar separation instead, or provide traffic advisories only.

items.situation: A parachute jump operation is about to begin in Class E airspace. You have 12 nonparticipating aircraft that will transit the jump airspace within the next 15 minutes. Individual transmissions to each aircraft would exceed your available time before the jump begins.
items.question_text: How should you handle advisories to the nonparticipating aircraft?

choices.text: Issue individual advisories to each aircraft regardless of the time constraints, even if it delays the jump.
Broadcast the advisory on appropriate control frequencies or ATIS when individual transmissions are impractical.
Skip advisories for aircraft more than 5 minutes from the jump area and only advise those closest.
Ask the jump aircraft to delay the operation until you can contact each nonparticipating aircraft individually.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-4c, when time or numbers of aircraft make individual transmissions impractical, advisories to nonparticipating aircraft may be broadcast on appropriate control frequencies or, when available, the ATIS broadcast. The broadcast alternative ensures all affected aircraft receive the safety information without delaying the jump operation.

items.front: When is ATC not required to maintain direct contact with a flight inspection pilot?
items.back: When 'otherwise agreed to' — an alternative coordination method has been established between the facility and the flight inspection unit.

items.front: When are special handling flight inspection operations coordinated with facilities?
items.back: Before departure. Operations requiring special participation of ground personnel, specific communications, or radar capabilities are pre-coordinated with appropriate facilities.

items.front: What timing standard applies when clearing a flight inspection aircraft according to pilot request?
items.back: As soon as practicable. The clearance does not need to be instantaneous, but it must be issued as soon as operational circumstances reasonably permit.

items.front: What is the sole exception that permits asking a flight inspection pilot to deviate from planned action?
items.back: To preclude an emergency situation. No other reason — not workload, not sequencing, not traffic flow — permits the controller to ask the pilot to deviate.

items.front: What unique requirement applies to commissioning flight checks that does not apply to other flight checks?
items.back: During commissioning flight checks, all SIDs, STARs, airways, DME fixes, and approaches must be flown. This is a comprehensive inspection of all procedures, not a selective one.

items.front: When a dangerous materials flight cannot accept a clearance amendment, what two things does the pilot advise ATC?
items.back: 1. Whether the traffic delay is acceptable. 2. Whether an alternate route or altitude is desired. ATC must then offer all possible assistance.

items.front: What must ATC do with information received about a stolen aircraft?
items.back: Forward it to the OS/CIC for reporting on the Domestic Events Network (DEN). This is a security reporting obligation separate from routine law enforcement assistance.

items.front: What authority does the originating ARTCC have regarding the 16-hour advance supersonic flight plan requirement?
items.back: The originating ARTCC may waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement. No other ARTCC or facility has this waiver authority.

items.front: When must the special use frequency NOT be assigned to single-pilot jet formations at night or in IMC?
items.back: When they will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations. The special-military-operations airspace designation overrides normal eligibility.

items.front: How are AWACS/NORAD Special flights identified in the flight plan?
items.back: The words 'AWACS/NORAD SPECIAL' appear as the first item in the remarks section of the flight plan. This placement ensures immediate recognition.

items.front: What is the total altitude block within which an evasive action maneuver must be confined?
items.back: 2,000 feet total — plus or minus 1,000 feet of the assigned flight level. The maneuver is performed at or above FL 250 along a 60 NM segment.

items.front: What must a controller do if a nonstandard formation breakup cannot be approved at the breakup fix?
items.back: Issue an appropriate clearance for the flight to continue as a formation. The formation stays together rather than transitioning to individual routes.

items.front: What two conditions must both be met when clearing an experimental aircraft that requires special handling?
items.back: 1. Clear according to pilot requests as traffic permits. 2. The clearance must not be contrary to ATC procedures.

items.front: When do the SUA/ATCAA/ALTRV separation procedures of Section 3 not apply at all?
items.back: They do not apply to Alert Areas or Controlled Firing Areas. These airspace types are explicitly excluded from Section 3 procedures.

items.front: What additional phrase must be included in a VFR-on-top clearance when the airspace is an ATCAA?
items.back: '(name of ATCAA) IS ATC ASSIGNED AIRSPACE.' This informs the pilot that the area is ATC-assigned, not standard special use airspace.

items.front: What two numbers define the IFR fuel-dumping altitude assignment rule?
items.back: At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the dump route or pattern. Both numbers must be satisfied — altitude above obstacle AND lateral scope.

items.front: How often are operators of unmanned free balloons required to record the balloon's position?
items.back: At least every two hours, as required by 14 CFR. Position reports are not forwarded to ATC unless ATC requests them.

items.front: What is the standard for authorizing parachute jumping in Class D airspace?
items.back: Authorize with respect to known or observed traffic. This is a more flexible standard than Class A/B/C, where jumping is authorized only within designated airspace.
